name: Appendicitis - Comprehensive Provider Query (Diagnosis + Procedure)
description: Simulates a surgeon who needs to identify all appendicitis patients for an outcomes study. The prompt explains that some patients may have an appendectomy procedure documented without a separate appendicitis diagnosis code, requiring the LLM to generate both a Condition query and a Procedure query. This is the hardest appendicitis test — it requires clinical reasoning about how appendicitis evidence can be captured in different FHIR resource types.
prompt: Find all patients with appendicitis. Search both diagnoses and appendectomy procedures to capture the complete population.

prompts.naive: Find all patients with appendicitis. Search both diagnoses and appendectomy procedures to capture the complete population.
prompts.expert:
I am conducting a surgical outcomes study on appendicitis and need to identify my complete appendicitis patient population. I know from experience that not all appendicitis patients have a formal diagnosis code in their chart — some patients went directly to surgery and only have an appendectomy procedure documented without a separate appendicitis diagnosis. Please run multiple FHIR queries to capture the full population:

1. Patients with an acute appendicitis diagnosis on their problem list
2. Patients who have had an appendectomy procedure performed

For each query, return the matching resources. I want to see the union of all patients found across both approaches to ensure I capture everyone.

metadata.algorithm_path_description: Comprehensive multi-query approach that mirrors how a real surgeon would identify the full appendicitis population. Requires the LLM to generate 2 separate FHIR queries (Condition + Procedure) and combine results. Tests clinical reasoning about procedure-only documentation patterns.

expected_query.notes: This test case expects MULTIPLE queries (see metadata.expected_queries). The expected_query.url is the minimum viable query. Evaluation unions patient IDs from both queries to get the complete appendicitis population.
